one_line_takeaway: Decomposing few-shot jailbreaking into low-perplexity "pattern learning" plus self-generated "behavior learning" demos, selected by greedy search, jailbreaks aligned open-source LLMs (including Llama-3/3.1) at ~90% sample-level ASR within 8 short shots while evading perplexity and SmoothLLM defenses.
key_finding: Self-Instruct-FSJ reaches roughly 90% sample-level ASR on advanced safety-aligned models including the Llama series within only 8 shots of concise demos (demo responses under 50 tokens). On the Llama-2 AdvBench subset it substantially outperforms all baselines (Table 5): Self-Instruct-FSJ 36.1% R-LVL / 90.0% S-LVL ASR versus GCG 16.1%/42.0%, PAP 1.9%/38.0%, PAIR 2.8%/6.0%, and AutoDAN 1.0%/10.0%; when GCG's target prefix is switched to "Hypothetically" its ASR drops to 6.5%/20.0%. Zero-shot demo generation alone (Table 1) yields >90% S-LVL ASR on every target model. The choice of "Hypothetically" is justified by its far lower conditional perplexity (Table 7, e.g. Llama-2: 813.7 with 0 patterns dropping to 1.4 at 8 patterns) versus "Sure" (9.3×10^11) and "Step" (3.4×10^12). Self-generated demos generally beat assistant-model demos because of lower target-model perplexity. The attack is also resilient to defenses (Table 4): perplexity and windowed-perplexity filters do not reduce ASR (e.g. 8-shot 36.1% R-LVL / 90.0% S-LVL unchanged), and SmoothLLM only partially degrades it, with swap perturbations even raising measured ASR.
core_contribution: The paper proposes Self-Instruct Few-Shot Jailbreaking (Self-Instruct-FSJ), an inference-time in-context jailbreaking attack on aligned open-source LLMs that improves on the prior I-FSJ method of Zheng et al. [52]. The authors' own contributions (distinguished from cited work) are: (1) using a hypothetical-scenario instruction suffix with "Hypothetically" as the target response prefix instead of I-FSJ's "Step", which preserves natural dialogue structure and has much lower conditional perplexity, avoiding the mismatched-generalization dependence of I-FSJ; (2) decomposing the attack into "pattern learning" (extending the suffix with replicated special-token co-occurrence patterns to lower the prefix perplexity) and "behavior learning" (sampling malicious demos directly from the target model itself by appending the target prefix to the generation query, rather than from a separate weakly-aligned assistant model); (3) a demo-level greedy search that selects demos sequentially by relative perplexity drop, replacing I-FSJ's random-initialization random-substitution search; and (4) perplexity-based filtering of demos. Note: GCG, AutoDAN, PAIR, PAP, I-FSJ, SmoothLLM, and the perplexity filter are cited prior work used as baselines/defenses, NOT this paper's contributions.
threat_model: White-box / gray-box inference-time attack on open-source aligned LLMs. The attacker needs the ability to modify the model's generation query (to append the target prefix and sample demos from the target model) and access to the last output logit (to compute conditional perplexity for demo-level greedy search); the model-specific special tokens must also be known. No fine-tuning or gradient access to full parameters is required. The paper explicitly notes (Appendix A) these requirements prevent application to closed-source models like GPT-4. Goal: elicit harmful/unethical content that safety alignment is meant to refuse.
limitations: Stated by the authors (Appendix A): the method requires knowledge of the target model's special tokens, full access to the generation query to sample self-generated demos, and the last output logit for greedy search — so it does not transfer to closed-source models like GPT-4. Additional scope limits evident from the text: all targets are 7–8B open-source models; evaluation subsets are small (50 cases each from AdvBench/HarmBench); ASR is judged solely by Llama-Guard-3-8B, and the authors themselves note swap perturbations can inflate ASR via gibberish flagged as unsafe. Tightening the perplexity filter beyond a point hurts attack performance by reducing demo diversity. No closed-source or larger-model results are reported.

datasets_used: AdvBench (harmful behaviors; full set for demo synthesis, 50-case random subset for evaluation)
quantitative_results: Self-Instruct-FSJ on Llama-2 AdvBench subset: 36.1% R-LVL / 90.0% S-LVL ASR (Table 5)
Baselines on Llama-2 (R-LVL/S-LVL): AutoDAN 1.0/10.0, PAIR 2.8/6.0, PAP 1.9/38.0, GCG 16.1/42.0, GCG* (Hypothetically target) 6.5/20.0
~90% S-LVL ASR on advanced LLMs including Llama series within 8 shots of demos <50 tokens
Zero-shot demo-generation S-LVL ASR >90% per model (Table 1): Llama-2 90.0, Llama-3 95.8, Llama-3.1 94.8, OpenChat-3.6 97.7, Qwen2.5 93.3, Starling-LM 97.1
Conditional target-prefix perplexity on Llama-2 (Table 7): Sure 9.3e11, Step 3.4e12, Hypothetically 813.7 (0 patterns) -> 7.6 (4) -> 1.4 (8) -> 1.0 (16)
Under perplexity filter on Llama-2 (Table 4, 8 shots): 36.1% R-LVL / 90.0% S-LVL unchanged vs undefended; SmoothLLM insert-10% drops to 15.1/56.0, patch-10% 31.6/82.0, swap-10% 49.0/96.0
Complexity to select 8 demos (Appendix B): demo-level greedy search ~O(2304·L) vs I-FSJ random search ~O(4096·L)
